prompt: All scenarios for the App Export Download feature in appsmith-v2 are committed and it's ready for the final review. The blueprint is files/blueprint.md, the system design is files/system-design.md, the full set of code changes is files/code-changes.diff, and the plan file is files/plan.md. Run the final review and produce the review report.
expectations: Produces a standalone review-report document that follows the prescribed structure exactly: a Summary section, an 'Orphan changes' section, a 'Scenario reviews' section with one subsection per scenario, a 'Missed corner cases' section, and a 'Recommended actions' section.

The Summary does a coverage check stating roughly 2 of 5 scenarios implemented and explicitly flags S3 (idempotent retry), S4 (ownership authorization), and S5 (assembly-failure terminal state) as unimplemented / having no associated code changes.

Detects the usage_analytics module (PostHog tracking + active_creators_daily rollup) as an orphan change that maps to no scenario in the blueprint, and calls it out as unrequested scope creep that should not be there.

For EACH scenario the review argues why the implementation is unacceptable with concrete reasons (adversarial framing), rather than praising or signing anything off; unimplemented scenarios are argued as unacceptable because nothing was built.

Cites specific kite-arch-compass principles behind the findings, including the inline zip assembly + DB access in the route handler violating thin-edges (Principle 2), layering (Principle 1), and do-slow-work-asynchronously (Principle 19); the missing idempotency for S3 (Principle 15); the missing server-side ownership check trusting a client-supplied workspace_id for S4 (least privilege Principle 27 and/or never-trust-input Principle 29); and the absent failed terminal state / non-resumable flow for S5 (Principle 16 and/or Principle 21).
Flags that the S2 download endpoint streams the artifact with no status==ready gate, so a failed or half-written export could be served as a valid download.

For at least the implemented scenarios, states the concrete impact ('because this is wrong, here is the consequence') AND writes a failure scenario as Gherkin (given/when/then) describing an input under which the implementation actually breaks.

Hunts missed corner cases BEYOND those the blueprint listed (Step 9) — e.g. app files changing mid-assembly, very large apps blocking the request thread / memory, or export-artifact retention and cleanup — not merely restating the blueprint's own S3-S5 corner cases.
Does NOT fix the code, rewrite the implementation, or re-trigger planning/implementation; any remediation appears only as advice under 'Recommended actions'.
